sentences.text: Just because I got those pictures that I see exactly what happened.
I even remember the colors of those rooms that we were.
And I just see, like, back-flash of those pictures.
Like I was telling you that the kapos were running, and this huge room that we were sitting in, waiting.
And one of the kapos came to me, probably because I was the smallest boy over there, and he gave me a piece of bread with some marmalade.
I wasn't hungry-- not yet-- and I didn't want to take it.
And my father said, Miki, take-- take it, because here we are in a prison.
And this had a terrible effect on me.
How come that my father is telling me that I am in a prison?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why am I in a prison?
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in a prison because of the clothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we understand that we are in a prison, very unusual prison.
But I still did not take from him the bread and the marmalade, because I didn't need it.
I thought that I don't need it.
I wasn't hungry.
No, even earlier, sure.
I know exac-- I know exactly why I am there.
It's because we are Jewish.
And there are only Jewish there.
I-- they-- of course they were Gentiles also.
But in this minute, I thought only it's just because that we are Jewish we are in this situation, sure.
OK, so then it was already evening, dark.
We were taken to-- back to Birkenau, to the camp, actually.
And my father was in block 19, I was in block 21, and my brother was in block 9.
So the first night, we arrived to this huge, wooden block.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we just couldn't understand what is happening.
Everything was so confused.
We were about 500 or 600 children in one block.
And some gypsy-- and I mean we were told a gypsy-- he didn't look like gypsy.
Those were German gypsies-- came and told us how to have to-- how we have to lay down where we are going to sleep, like sardines, even numbers this way and uneven numbers this way.
And we had to lay down on this cement floor, which was OK.
It wasn't cold.
It was June.
But we just couldn't fall asleep.

Then I remember next night, they found out some different way to go-- to-- to go sleep where-- we were-- the first one was sitting by the wall, and we were sitting-- I don't know how to explain-- like-- like in a sitting position.
And then they told us to lay down on the left side.
So all the 10 or 12 children, to lay down on the left side.
It was impossible to fall asleep like this.
So at night we were pushing and shoving each other.
It was an impossible situation.
We didn't know from the first couple days what is happening to us.
But--
They were up to 16.
Up to 16.
I was between the youngest, 13, 14, yeah.
[INAUDIBLE]
No younger--
No younger.
No younger.
No, all boys.
All boys.
And all from Slovakia and from Hungaria.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so this was the situation in the block.
My-- and they wake us up very early in the morning.
And early in the morning in this part of Poland, even in June, is very, very cold.
And we had only those pajama-like clothing.
So at 6:00 in the morning, we were thrown out from our barracks.
So we were sitting together, close to each other, and waiting for breakfast, because by then we were already very hungry.
So the breakfast was some dark colored, like, black-colored tea, sweet tea.
But we were told this later that this tea was made from some sweet herbs.
So the tea was the breakfast.
Then we were waiting.
We didn't do absolutely nothing, just waiting.
Then we were waiting for lunch.
The lunch was brought in wooden, square, like, garbage cans.
Four people were bringing the lunch.
And they were giving us this-- this-- the lunch was a soup, all kind of-- everything mixed together, rice and-- and beans and some potatoes.
And everything just cooked together, but without salt.
Very, very un-tasty.
Because I didn't care.
When you are hungry, you want to eat everything.
But salt bothered us.
So during the time in Auschwitz, we found some reddish colored salt in the bathrooms.
But it smelled terrible.
But the taste was salt.
Salt.
We didn't know how come the food didn't have no salt.
But later on I found out, because of the war, even the Germans, they didn't have no salt.
So the food-- the food was like that, like for pigs, like for animals.
And then again in the evening, waiting for the ration of bread, a piece bread from the beginning for 12 people.
It's like-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was a whole operation to-- to cut for 12 people this heavy bread.
And it didn't even-- it looked like bread, but didn't tasted like bread.
And it was sawdust all over from outside.
You could have see and feel when you eat, it was sawdust.
And inside was very sticky.
Every day somebody else.
We-- we were always together.
Let's see, if 12 people, so 12 best friends.
We took the bread, and everyday, somebody else took off his shirt to cut the bread on the shirt.
Why?
Because of the crumbs, that they were fallen from the bread belonged to the person who, this day, was giving his shirt.
I mean, the-- his blouse, whatever.
So every day somebody else had the privilege to cut the bread.
And we were fighting.
We were fighting for the-- for the bigger portion of bread.
And we-- we made all kinds of crazy things to be sure that everybody's getting exactly what he was-- it was like a lottery.
You know, it was like a lottery.
Today you are deciding who's going to get this piece of bread.
Tomorrow, someone else.
And then slowly, slowly, one day we received 18 people the same size of bread.
And it was very, very tough.
No, from the beginning it was everything verbal.
Everything verbal.
Just pushing and shoving, but no fights.
We were still civilized and friends.
In the beginning, that I spent only, I think, six weeks in Auschwitz-- six or seven weeks-- we were still normal people, but behaved already strange because of the hunger.
Yes.
Yes.
As a matter of fact, a month later that we came to Auschwitz, my father was in the block 19, just next to me.
But our block was quarantined, barbed wire.
Nobody was able to get out or come in, because we had some sickness between the children.
When we arrived, they were-- there were already a lot of children.
And they had this [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know how you call it in English, but those little lumps between the fingers, very itchy.
It's from filth.
I don't know if you heard this.
So because of this, we were not allowed to move to other blocks, other-- other barracks.
So my father was next to me.
I saw him almost all day.
And-- just the barbed wire-- but it was barbed wire, not electrical barbed wire.
Just barbed wire.
We couldn't leave the barracks.
I mean, the-- the courtyard-- the court was free to move around, but no other places.
My father was in block 19, like I said.
My brother was in block 9.
He was, about a month later, like I told you, they-- the took them-- they take-- they concentrate some 1,000 older people.
I mean older-- you know, strong people, able people-- and they were taken to Dachau.
I didn't know, and they didn't know.
I just found out later where they were taken.
And my father exchanged someone so my brother was able to go with him, because he was in the block from youngsters, because he was 16 years old.
So my father and my brother left beginning of July-- beginning of July-- for Dachau.
And we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hugging each other and kissing each other before they left, and I was happy that I was able to see my brother go with my father.
I know, of course, I know I couldn't go because I was in the quarantine block.
But my father, before he told me goodbye, he told me, Miki, if you have a chance, any possibility to get out from here, try to leave.
And this, I took very seriously, because he actually ordered me.
Miki, like, you must leave this place, because probably he knew what is happening in Auschwitz.
You understand?
No, we didn't know.
We didn't know.
One day they took us out for-- for a long walk, they said.
And we were walking towards the Hungarian C lager, the Hungarian woman's camp.
We were walking.
And from then everyone grabbed, next to the camp, grabbed the-- you know how they cut the grass, square piece?
So everybody grabbed a piece of or two of this grass squares, and we brought them to the SS barracks.
So this was the only day that ever I left the barrack, and the only-- only day that we were able to see other prisoners.
And while we were walking next to the woman's camp, we were yelling the names of the cities.
Because we wouldn't recognize no one.
The girls were all shaved.
But we were yelling the names, and I was yelling Levice.
And a little girl was running towards.
She says, I am from Levice.
Who are you?
I said, Miki Popik.
She says your cousin is here, Magda.
And I saw Magda.
It was very-- I-- I-- I touched her hand through the barbed wire.
It was very dangerous.
We had to put it this way because the opening was about 18 inches, but also 18 inches from the other side.
So the other wire was only nine inch.
So we-- if I put between the electric wire my hands this way, we were able to shake hands.
It was dangerous, but it was very important.
God knows.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I never saw her again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never saw her again.
She was 15 years old, very beautiful little girl.
Not little girl, she was already 15, a beautiful girl.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So anyway, like I said, my father told me to leave.
And two weeks later-- should I continue?
Two weeks later, a truck came with German soldiers with Mengele.
And by then, we already knew who is Mengele.
We were terrible scared when we heard the name Mengele.

We know that Mengele for us was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was the name frightening, because they told us that he's taking children and he's deciding who goes to work and who doesn't go to work, who gets burned.
But we didn't believe that they are burning.
Even if we were told, we did not believe that people are burned or gassed.
We did not believe that.
In almost two months in Auschwitz-- seven weeks-- I heard it daily, but we couldn't believe it.
[INAUDIBLE] or maybe we just couldn't comprehend that it is a-- that it's possible to burn children.
But a day before I left Auschwitz-Birkenau, at night, we were awoken by a terrible yelling and crying and-- it was a terrible-- the sounds were unbelievable.
The gypsy camp was taken to the crematorias.
Now, the gypsy camp was a camp in Birkenau.
Our uneven numbers, we were the Hungarian transport, and the gypsies were in the even numbers, just across the street.
Street-- just across the barrack.
Those were all gypsies.
When I saw the gypsies, they were families living together, not like we.
They were not wearing the striped clothing that we had.
They were wearing civil clothing.
They looked different.
I remember the gypsies from Slovakia, they were different than we were.
But those gypsies, they were good-looking people-- not that the gypsies are not good-looking-- but they did look nothing like gypsies.
I don't even know why they called them gypsies.
But those were German gypsies.
They were mixed together with Germans.
I even saw blonde gypsies.
They were with their hair.
And those were taken at night, and they were cremated, 18,000 of them.
We were told in the morning that the gypsies were taken, and they were killed.
So they let us out from our barracks, and we went into the gypsy barracks.
And we saw that they lived differently than we.
They had heavy-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, the heavy bed covers.
They were also laying on wooden floors.
And they had an enormous amount of mustard everywhere.
Food we couldn't find, very little.
But they had mustered everywhere.
Later on I found out that they were given the mustard to-- to find out what kind of effect has mustard on people if they eat a lot of mustard.
This what the Hungarian doctors told me in Mauthausen later on, because I told them that we just grabbed jars of mustard and I was eating.
I would eat anything.
So we had mustard.
So we ate mustard.
Next day, I was selected to go to a work camp.
Yes.
Yeah, next day, exactly next day that the gypsies were taken, afternoon, we got ordered, Appel.
Mengele by himself.
Yes.
Probably, sure.
Probably they were taken to the gas chambers.
And the yellings and the cryings were because they were in-- in Auschwitz for a couple years already, in Birkenau.
So they knew what it means when they are to be taken.
We didn't know.
Yes.
Sure, we even smelled.
We smelled.
But to me, we-- we were still believing that it's-- it's not human beings that they are burned.
The chimneys were huge.
And during the day, you saw the heavy smoke.
At night, in the evening, you could have seen fire above the chimney.
The smell was terrible smell, but we didn't know.
I never smelled before human flesh.
Never in my life.
So I didn't know what is it.
Well, during the day was grayish-- grayish black, grayish brownish.
And at night, we didn't saw the smoke, but we saw the fire from the chimneys.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we got called roll call-- Appell.
And two-- two small German trucks-- army trucks-- came.
And they parked front of the barrack with German Shepherds, few German soldiers.
And all of a sudden Mengele showed up.
I don't know if he drove or he walked.
He just showed up.
And he started to talk German.
I understand German.
And he was asking, who wants to work something with metal?
My father ordered me to leave, so I raised my hand.
But then, a lot of children raised their hands.
Then he asked, who was already working with things-- with metal?
So I thought, I was a mechanic since I was born.
I'm going to raise the hand.
What can happen?
Of course I wasn't a mechanic.
I never worked in my life. 13 years old boy.
So I raised my hand, and I was in the front row.
And we were always standing friends together, because if they count and they cut us, they should be-- we should be always together.
So best friends, we always stayed together.
And five, because I was the shortest-- I-- I still am-- I was standing in the front.
And I decided to raise my hand.
And my friends were angry.
Miki, why are you doing that?
So I told them, I have to leave here.
I have to leave.
Even though they didn't left, maybe some of them behind me picked up their hands, but I didn't see it.
I saw only myself.
I mean, I saw other boys also.
And we were 600 children in one line.
And Mengele was walking and selecting.
And I was probably middle of the 600 children.
There were other children in the block 23, also 600.
So-- but our row was the first one.
And when he passed by me, he took, already, three or four children.
But they were all older.
I saw it, and bigger than I was.
Yes, I was a small child.
Short.
Short, but strong.
So I was on the-- and also between very young.
So I raised my hand, and he approached me, and I looked at him in his eyes.
And he was a tall man, and I just looked at him, and he passed by me.
But he continued to look at me, and I looked at him.
And he stepped back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I won't forget this never in my life.
And he hit me over my face.
And he says, [GERMAN], like you do to a dog.
And I felt good.
It's like I-- I wanted to tell him, I have to go.
And he stepped back and he hit me over the face.
And I don't think Mengele stepped back many times in his life.
He just selected me.
I was the smallest between the 14 children.
No.
I was selected by-- he just showed with a-- with his usual stick.
But when he selected me, he-- he hit me, like, in a friendly way, not in a-- like a dog you hit.
And he told me [GERMAN].
It's like go, jump.
So you caught his-- his glance.
Somehow you-- something-- you connected.
Yes.
Yes.
And I felt that it was very important for me.
And they took us to another barrack, and they gave us those numbers.
And the minute they gave me the number, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I liked it.
I really-- I was happy about it.
I don't know why, but to me, this was something very important.
I was very happy.
It was, to me, like a passport, to me that I'm going to do something.
I was given the number because they wanted me.
I-- it was a very strange feelings, that I know that I am leaving from this hell.
I knew it, that I'm leaving the hell.
And after they gave us this number, they told us, you can go back to your barrack to say hello to your friends for 20 minutes.
And I went back, and my friends were upset with me that I aban-- that I left them.
My real close friends.
We were already like brothers.
Like a family, we were already, in this six, seven weeks.
We were helping each other.
We were trying to comfort each other, and to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt bad, mixed.
I feel good for one hand that I got this number.
But I felt bad that I had to leave them, and I'm going with people that I don't know.
[GERMAN] -- B6193.
Yeah, OK.
